coredata.dc:title: A label-free photoelectrochemical aptasensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for chloramphenicol determination
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yong
Yan, Kai
Okoth, Otieno Kevin
Zhang, Jingdong
coredata.dc:description: A photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform for chloramphenicol (CAP) detection was constructed using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as transducer species and label-free aptamer as biological recognition element. N-GQDs, synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal method, were explored to achieve highly efficient photon-to-electricity conversion under visible light irradiation. The obtained N-GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which displayed a narrow size distribution with a mean diameter of 2.14nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) analysis confirmed that nitrogen was successfully doped in GQDs. The UV–visible absorption spectra indicated that nitrogen doping obviously enhanced the absorption of GQDs in visible light region. As a result, the PEC activity of GQDs was promoted by nitrogen doping. Additionally, the π-conjugated structure of N-GQDs provided an excellent platform for aptamer immobilization via π–π stacking interaction. Such an aptamer/N-GQDs based sensor showed a linear PEC response to CAP concentration in the range of 10–250nM with a detection limit (3 S/N) of 3.1nM. The developed PEC aptasensor exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, good reproducibility and high stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoelectrochemical sensor
Aptamer
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Chloramphenicol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impedance spectroscopic analysis of sensor fabrication
Optimizing response of aptasensor to CAP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 61172005 ). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for help in the characterization of materials. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) XPS survey spectrum, and (C) FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs. Inset of (A): HR-TEM image of N-GQDs.

(A) Photocurrent responses of (a) N-GQDs/FTO, (b) FTO, and (c) GQDs/FTO electrodes. (B) Influence of N-GQDs concentration on the photocurrent response of N-GQDs modified electrodes. The PEC measurements were recorded in 0.1M Na2SO4 at a bias potential of +0.8V.
Influences of (A) aptamer concentration and (B) bias potential on PEC response of aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO toward 200nM CAP. The PEC measurements were recorded in 0.1M Na2SO4.

Photocurrent responses of aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO toward CAP at different concentrations: (a) 0, (b) 10, (c) 30, (d) 60, (e) 100, (f) 150, (g) 200, (h) 250nM. The PEC measurements were recorded in 0.1M Na2SO4 at a bias potential of +0.8V. Inset: calibration plot.
PEC responses of aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO toward various antibiotics at 200nM. The PEC measurements were recorded in 0.1M Na2SO4 at a bias potential of +0.8V.
Comparison of different methods for CAP determination.

head.ce:title: A label-free photoelectrochemical aptasensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for chloramphenicol determination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Large-Format Battery Materials and System (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Luoyu Road 1037, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Large-Format Battery Materials and System (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensing platform for chloramphenicol (CAP) detection was constructed using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as transducer species and label-free aptamer as biological recognition element. N-GQDs, synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal method, were explored to achieve highly efficient photon-to-electricity conversion under visible light irradiation. The obtained N-GQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which displayed a narrow size distribution with a mean diameter of 2.14nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FT-IR) analysis confirmed that nitrogen was successfully doped in GQDs. The UV–visible absorption spectra indicated that nitrogen doping obviously enhanced the absorption of GQDs in visible light region. As a result, the PEC activity of GQDs was promoted by nitrogen doping. Additionally, the π-conjugated structure of N-GQDs provided an excellent platform for aptamer immobilization via π–π stacking interaction. Such an aptamer/N-GQDs based sensor showed a linear PEC response to CAP concentration in the range of 10–250nM with a detection limit (3 S/N) of 3.1nM. The developed PEC aptasensor exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, good reproducibility and high stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Chloramphenicol (CAP) is a broad-spectrum phenicol antibiotic active against infections caused by gram-positive and gram-negative microorganisms. However, CAP is suspected to be carcinogenic and has potential lethal side effects on human beings, such as leukemia, aplastic anemia and gray baby syndrome (Wilson and Cockerill, 1987), and thus it is mainly limited to be used as a low-priced and effective veterinary drug (Xu et al., 2012). Due to the effluent from livestock wastes, CAP has been frequently detected in rivers, municipal sewage and sediment, which may cause ecological risks (Choi et al., 2008; Liu et al., 2009). For instance, the occurrence of CAP at 5.35–7.52nM was reported in wastewater samples (Peng et al., 2006). Moreover, CAP residues have been found in various animal-derived foods such as meat, egg, honey and milk (Xu et al., 2012). The minimum required performance limit (MRPL) for CAP residues in food of animal origin was set to be 0.3μgkg− 1 by the European Union (EU) (Karaseva and Ermolaeva, 2012). To meet the demand of low detection limit in environmental waters and food, it is necessary to develop highly sensitive and selective analytical approaches to CAP determination. Various instrumental methods including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Chen et al., 2009), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Posyniak et al., 2003), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Rodziewicz and Zawadzka, 2008), immunoassay (Impens et al., 2003) and chemiluminescence (David et al., 2000) have been established for CAP determination. However, these methods usually require expensive equipments and time-consuming operation procedures. It is therefore of great significance to develop simple, rapid and low-cost methods for sensitive determination of CAP.

Owing to the merits of high sensitivity, simple equipment, cost-effectiveness, and ease of miniaturization, photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors have attracted increasing research interest in recent years (Zhao et al., 2014). Generally, PEC sensors are fabricated on photoactive electrodes which can convert photoirradiation to electrical signal; hence, photoactive materials are crucial for the performance of the PEC sensors (Wang et al., 2014a). Among the various photoactive materials, TiO2 and ZnO are the two most widely used semiconductors showing many advantages such as high photocatalytic activity, good stability, non-toxicity and low cost (Shankar et al., 2009; Tarwal and Patil, 2011). Unfortunately, their applications in devices driven by visible light are limited, owing to their wide band-gap energies. By contrast, narrow band-gap photoactive materials such as CdS, CdSe and C3N4 can efficiently absorb visible light (Liu et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2014; Li et al., 2014b). Using these visible light-active materials instead of wide band-gap semiconductors in PEC sensing may offer two advantages: (1) efficient use of the visible range of the solar energy; and (2) avoiding high background arising from the direct UV photolysis of analytes.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, are single atom-thick graphene sheets with sizes smaller than 10nm. Similar to graphene nanosheets, GQDs possess excellent properties such as high surface area and good surface grafting by using the π–π conjugated network or abundant surface functional groups (Lee et al., 2013; Ju and Chen, 2014). Recently, GQDs have ignited much interest owing to their robust chemical inertness, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, high dispersibility in water, and stable photoluminescence (Li et al., 2014c). GQDs have been extensively employed in photocatalysis, bioimaging, ion detection, and electrochemical luminescence (Baker and Baker 2010; Li et al., 2012a; Safavi et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2013). On the other hand, many reports have demonstrated that doping carbon nanomaterials with heteroatoms can effectively tune their intrinsic properties including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features (Li et al., 2012c; Qu et al., 2013). Since nitrogen has a comparable atomic size to carbon and can provide five valence electrons for bonding with carbon atoms, it has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon materials (Tam et al., 2014). Nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) exhibiting extraordinary physical and chemical properties have been synthesized and widely explored for fluorescent sensing (Ju and Chen, 2014; Tam et al., 2014; Cai et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2015). Additionally, N-GQDs have been used as excellent electrode materials in electrocatalysis (Li et al., 2012b) and electrochemiluminescence (Du et al., 2015).

As well-known antibody mimetics possessing high recognition ability to specific targets, aptamers have been extensively utilized to construct highly selective biosensors, in cooperation with optical, thermal, mass and electrochemical transducers (Lee et al., 2008). Recently, PEC detection has been coupled with aptasensing. Using thrombin as a target analyte, a highly sensitive PEC aptasensor was fabricated based on a layer-by-layer assembly of functionalized graphene and CdSe nanoparticles (Zhang et al., 2011). The PEC aptasensor for thrombin detection was improved by using electroactive mediator as signal enhancer (Li et al., 2015). Moreover, highly sensitive PEC aptasensors for specific detection of various analytes such as adenosine triphosphate (ATP) (Wang et al., 2014c), SK-BR-3 cancer cells (Liu et al., 2014), kanamycin (Li et al., 2014b) and Ag+ (Li et al., 2014a) were successfully constructed.

In the present work, we employed N-GQDs as visible light-responsive materials to develop a novel PEC biosensor for highly sensitive determination of CAP. A low-cost label-free CAP-binding aptamer was designed as a biorecognition element. The large specific surface area and the π-conjugated structure of N-GQDs ensured the facile immobilization of aptamer on N-GQDs through π-π stacking interaction between the nucleobases and N-GQDs (Antony and Grimme, 2008; Ortmann et al., 2005). When CAP was present in solution, it could be captured by aptamer, which then reacted with photogenerated holes on N-GQDs to produce an enhanced photocurrent signal. Thus, a PEC aptasensor exhibiting a highly sensitive and selective response to CAP was fabricated.

Citric acid (CA), urea, NaOH, NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chloamphenicol (CAP), kanamycin, oxytetracycline, doxycycline, chlorotetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and neomycin sulfate were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CAP eye drops were from Changchun Dirui pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Changchun, China). All other reagents of analytical grade were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The DNA aptamer for CAP was synthesized by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) with the following sequence: 5′-ACTTC AGTGA GTTGT CCCAC GGTCG GCGAG TCGGT GGTAG-3′. The stock solution of oligonucleotides was prepared in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.01M, pH 7.4). Ultrapure water with a specific resistivity>18.2MΩcm was used throughout the investigation.

Water-soluble N-GQDs were prepared based on a reported procedure (Jiang et al., 2014). Briefly, 0.525g CA (2.5mmol) and 0.45g urea (7.5mmol) were mixed with 12mL water. The mixture was then transferred into a 50mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated to 160°C in an electric oven for 8h, after which the reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The final product was collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuging at 5000rpm for 5min. The product was dispersed into water to prepare different concentrations of N-GQDs solutions. For comparison, GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing CA as described previously (Dong et al., 2012).

Before fabrication, F-doped SnO2 (FTO) conducting glass (Dalian Heptachroma SolarTech Co., Ltd., China) was cleaned by successive sonication in acetone and ethanol for 30min, followed by dipping in saturated NaOH aqueous solution for 30min. Subsequently, the FTO glass was rinsed with deionized water and dried with highly pure nitrogen gas. The surface of FTO (exposed geometry area of 0.096cm2) was treated with 10µL of 2% PDDA solution containing 0.5M NaCl to make the electrode surface positively charged (Yan et al., 2014). After being thoroughly rinsed with distilled water and dried with nitrogen gas, the FTO surface was coated with a certain amount of N-GQDs solution and dried at 70°C for 60min. The obtained N-GQDs/FTO electrode was coated with 8µL of aptamer at a desired concentration (diluted with 0.01M PBS), and then dried at 60°C for 120min to ensure the effective immobilization of aptamer on N-GQDs. It was then thoroughly rinsed with PBS and dried with nitrogen gas.

The surface morphology was characterized by a TecnaiG2 F30 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (FEI, Netherlands) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured on a 5300 ESCA instrument (Perkin-Elmer PHI Co., USA) using an Al Kα X-ray source at a power of 250W. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with an Equinox 55 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker Co., Germany). The UV–visible absorption spectra were measured on a TU-1900 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., China). Electrochemical impedance analysis was carried out with a CHI660A electrochemical working station (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., China). The concentration of CAP determined by HPLC was performed on an Agilent (USA) 1100 module system equipped with a C18 column (150mm×4.6mm). The mobile phase was a mixture of methanol: water (65:35, v/v), and the flow rate was 1mLmin−1. The UV detection wavelength was set at 278nm and the column temperature was 25°C.

The as-prepared aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrode was incubated in 0.01M PBS containing a desired concentration of CAP at 60°C for 60min. After being thoroughly rinsed with PBS, the aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrode was put into a three-electrode cell containing 0.1M Na2SO4 solution to serve as the working electrode. A platinum wire and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) acted as the auxiliary and reference electrodes, respectively. The photocurrent was measured with a CHI830C electrochemical working station (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., China). A PLS-SXE 300 xenon lamp (Perfect Co., China) (λ>420nm) was used as the visible light source. The distance between the light source and working electrode was 10cm.

The as-prepared N-GQDs were characterized by TEM, XPS and FT-IR ( Fig. 1). Fig. 1A shows the TEM image of N-GQDs. Uniformly dispersed N-GQDs in a narrow size distribution were observed. And the mean diameter of N-GQDs was estimated to be 2.14nm. Furthermore, the HR-TEM image (Inset of Fig. 1A) revealed the high crystallinity of N-GQDs. A lattice spacing of 0.23nm was exhibited, which was assigned to (1120) lattice fringes of graphene (Ju et al., 2014).

Analysis of elemental composition of the prepared N-GQDs was performed using XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1B, three predominant peaks around 284eV, 400eV and 533eV corresponding respectively to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s were clearly observed, showing the presence of C, O and N in the sample. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s could be fitted with five peaks at 284.1eV, 284.6eV, 286.4eV, 288.0eV and 288.7eV (Fig. S1). These peaks were assigned to the sp2 carbon (C=C and C–C) in graphene, the sp3 carbon (C–O/C–N), the C=O in carboxylic and O–C=O groups, respectively (Xu et al., 2015; Feng et al., 2014). On the other hand, the analysis of N 1s XPS spectrum showed two peaks at 399.7 and 401.5eV (Fig. S2) which were attributed to the pyrrolic N (C–N–C) and graphitic N (N-(C)3) bands, respectively (Qu et al., 2014). These results confirmed that nitrogen atoms were successfully doped in the framework of GQDs.

The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized N-GQDs is presented in Fig. 1C. The characteristic absorption peaks for C=C at 1571cm−1 and C–C at 1234cm− 1 were observed, indicating the existence of delocalized π-electrons in N-GQDs molecules (Lu et al., 2013). The absorption peaks around 3205cm− 1 and 1040cm− 1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of N–H and N–C groups, respectively (Hu et al., 2013). This result further confirmed the doping of N in the as-prepared sample. Moreover, three absorption peaks appeared at 1425cm− 1, 1665cm− 1and 1109cm− 1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of COO−, C=O and C–O groups, respectively (Zhu et al., 2014). These hydrophilic groups could stabilize N-GQDs in aqueous media.

The photocurrent response of N-GQDs immobilized on FTO was studied in 0.1M Na2SO4 solution by applying an anodic bias potential of +0.8V ( Fig. 2A). It was observed that the N-GQDs/FTO electrode exhibited a sensitive response to the irradiation of visible light. When the lamp was turned on, the current recorded on N-GQDs/FTO was rapidly promoted from 0.06μA to 0.16μA within 1s and then to 0.20μA at 10s. The current slowly increased to 0.22μA when the irradiation time was further prolonged from 10s to 20s. Considering that the promotion of current by irradiation was mostly obtained at the initial period of 10s while short responsive time was preferred for PEC sensing, we calculated the photocurrent of ca. 0.14μA for the first 10s to represent the response of N-GQDs/FTO electrode. By contrast, the FTO substrate showed a negligible photocurrent response. When GQDs was used instead of N-GQDs, the photocurrent response was ca. 0.07μA, which was almost half that of N-GQDs/FTO, indicating that nitrogen doping could improve the PEC activity of GQDs. Moreover, the response of GQDs/FTO quickly increased to a steady current under visible light irradiation, which was different from that of N-GQDs/FTO. Such a difference implied that the PEC responsive characteristic of GQDs was changed by nitrogen doping. According to UV–visible spectroscopic analysis, the absorption of GQDs in visible light region was obviously promoted by nitrogen doping (Fig. S3). Therefore, the apparent visible light absorption endues N-GQDs with higher PEC activity.

Furthermore, the influence of the concentration of N-GQDs used for electrode modification on the photocurrent response was investigated (Fig. 2B). It was observed that the photocurrent increased with increasing the concentration of N-GQDs from 1.0gL− 1 to 2.0gL− 1. This result is in agreement with the fact that more N-GQDs are able to provide more photogenerated holes and electrons to participate in the PEC process. However, the photocurrent response was declined when the concentration of N-GQDs reached 2.5gL− 1. It is likely that excessive N-GQDs can act as charge recombination centers (Wang et al., 2014b) in which the recombination of electrons and holes is promoted. When the concentration of N-GQDs was further increased to 3.0gL− 1, the photocurrent response was slightly enhanced, implying that the influence of photogenerated electrons provided by N-GQDs was more competitive than that of charge recombination centers. Thus, 2.0gL− 1 N-GQDs showing the highest photocurrent were used for fabricating the PEC sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impedance spectroscopic analysis of sensor fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor was fabricated by successively immobilizing photoactive materials (N-GQDs) and biorecognition element (aptamer) on FTO substrate surface. Considering the distinct conducting properties of N-GQDs and aptamer, we studied the interfacial behavior of FTO, N-GQDs/FTO and aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrodes by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox probe. Fig. S4 shows the Nyquist plots of EIS recorded on various modified electrodes in 0.1M KCl solution containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The semicircle diameter of Nyquist plot represents the interfacial charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of electrode. The R ct value of FTO electrode was estimated to be 606Ω (curve a in Fig. S4), which was reduced to 400Ω after FTO was coated with N-GQDs (curve b in Fig. S4). The smaller R ct value indicated that high conductivity of N-QGDs facilitated the electron transfer between [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− reaction species and electrode. However, the R ct value was drastically increased to 1847Ω after aptamer was immobilized on N-GQDs/FTO (curve c in Fig. S4). Such a high R ct value could be attributed to the fact that aptamer molecules carrying negative charges induced electrostatic repulsion between the electrode surface and negatively charged redox species of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 − (Li et al., 2014b). These results confirmed the effective immobilization of the aptamer on N-GQDs modified electrode via π–π stacking interaction (Antony and Grimme, 2008; Ortmann et al., 2005).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing response of aptasensor to CAP
ce:section.ce:para: The response of the fabricated aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrode toward CAP was recorded in 0.1M Na2SO4 by applying an anodic bias potential of +0.8V. It was observed that the photocurrent of aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO increased when CAP was present in electrolyte, confirming that aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO could be used as a sensor for CAP detection. To quantitatively evaluate the response of the sensor to CAP, the difference of photocurrent before and after incubation with CAP (ΔPI) was calculated as the net PEC response. The response was obviously influenced by the concentration of aptamer immobilized on the electrode. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the PEC response toward CAP markedly increased with increasing the aptamer concentration up to 2µM, suggesting that higher concentration of aptamer immobilized on the electrode could capture more CAP molecules. However, when the aptamer concentration exceeded 2µM, the PEC response declined. This result can be explained by the steric hindrance created by excessive aptamer leading to blockage of electrons transfer (Yan et al., 2012). Consequently, aptamer at 2µM was chosen for fabricating the sensor.

Meanwhile, the bias potential showed an obvious effect on the PEC response of the sensor (Fig. 3B). The response was found to be increased with increasing the bias potential from +0.5 to +0.8V. This result is consistent with the fact that larger anodic bias potential would drive more photo-generated electrons to the counter electrode and suppress the recombination of hole-electron pairs more effectively, leading to higher photocurrent (Liu et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2014). However, the response toward CAP did not show further enhancement at applied potential higher than +0.8V, owing to the saturation of photo-generated holes consumed by CAP. Thus, +0.8V was used as the optimum applied potential for PEC sensing.

In addition, we compared the PEC responses of aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO and aptamer/GQDs/FTO toward CAP under the same experimental conditions. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the aptasensor fabricated on N-GQDs/FTO exhibited higher PEC response toward CAP than that immobilized on GQDs/FTO, consistent with the aforementioned higher PEC activity of N-GQDs. This demonstrates the superiority of N-GQDs over GQDs in fabrication of highly sensitive PEC aptasensor.

The developed aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrode was applied to the quantitative determination of CAP in 0.1M Na2SO4 solution. Fig. 4 displays the photocurrent responses of the proposed aptamer sensor to CAP at different concentrations. The photocurrent response increased with increasing the concentrations of CAP, attributed to capture of more CAP molecules participating in the PEC process. The PEC response of the sensor was found to be linearly proportional to CAP concentration in the range of 10nM–250nM. The linear regression equation was expressed as ΔPI/μA=0.000235C/nM+0.0049 with a correlation coefficient of 0.998. The detection limit (3 S/N) was estimated to be 3.1nM. Compared with many previously reported methods for CAP determination ( Table 1), this PEC sensor showed lower detection limit and wider linear range. Such a low detection limit can not only be satisfactory for assay of CAP in pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids, but also be particularly useful for the rapid determination of trace CAP in environmental waters and CAP residues in food.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of this PEC aptamer sensor, the PEC responses toward various antibiotics such as chloramphenicol, kanamycin, oxytetracycline, doxycycline, chlorotetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and neomycin sulfate were studied ( Fig. 5). Negligible responses on the PEC aptamer sensor were observed for other antibiotics as compared with CAP. The high selectivity of this sensor is due to the specific interaction of aptamer and target CAP molecules. To examine the reproducibility of PEC aptamer sensor, the responses of five independently prepared aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrodes toward 200nM CAP were studied. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.6%, meaning a good reproducibility. Moreover, the aptamer/N-GQDs/FTO electrode stored at 4°C did not show obvious change in response after two weeks, indicating the high stability of the sensor.

To check the applicability of the aptasensor, a real sample of CAP eye drops was chosen for assaying the content of CAP using the proposed PEC method. The result (Table S1) showed that the content of CAP in the sample was 7.12mM. At the same time, the content of CAP determined by the conventional HPLC method was 7.18mM. The consistence of the two results confirms the feasibility of the proposed method for CAP detection in the real sample.

In this work, we developed a visible light PEC aptasensor based on N-GQDs for CAP determination. The nitrogen doping remarkably improved the PEC performance of N-GQDs as transducer species. Due to π–π stacking interaction, aptamer was effectively anchored on N-GQDs without any modification. Thus, a high performance PEC aptamer sensor which displayed highly sensitive and selective response toward CAP was constructed. Moreover, the sensor exhibited good reproducibility and high stability. This N-GQDs-based PEC aptamer sensor not only provides a new sensitive strategy for CAP detection, but also expands the application of N-GQDs to PEC sensing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 61172005). The authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for help in the characterization of materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.07.067.
